summary: Justin Brennan conducts a live deal analysis of a 42-unit multifamily property in Logansport, Indiana with mentee Michael, focusing on market cap rates, underwriting assumptions, and tertiary market considerations. The discussion covers realistic exit cap rates (7-7.5% vs. seller's 7.75%), expense ratios, debt service coverage ratios, and due diligence priorities for small-market deals.
takeaways: Run tertiary market deals at 6.5% minimum exit cap rate for safety, even if market data shows 7-8%
Stress test assumptions: use realistic 5% credit loss rather than seller's optimistic numbers
Verify historical cap rates using ChatGPT data aggregation across multiple real estate websites
Check for pipeline of new construction units in development department over next 2 years to assess downward pressure
Tertiary market lending rates typically run high fives to middle-low sixes (currently improving toward sub-5%)
For small towns, prioritize job market diversity: need 6+ strong employers, not single-employer dependency like 'one Walmart'
Due diligence must include historical occupancy verification and confirmation market hasn't changed since seller's T12 projections

skill_candidates.change: Apply minimum 6.5% cap rate floor for tertiary market exit underwriting rather than matching broker's optimistic assumptions
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin's direct guidance: 'from a safe perspective, I would run it at a six and a half, right?' and later 'I wouldn't go any lower than a seven' when discussing Logan's Port, Indiana deal

skill_candidates.change: Cross-verify seller's expense ratios and credit loss percentages using stress-tested assumptions before accepting proforma
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin noted seller used 'glowing assumptions' with 5% credit loss, then walked through stress-testing the numbers with Michael to validate 7.4-7.6% realistic cap rate vs. seller's 7.75%

skill_candidates.change: Document employer diversity and job market stability for small markets using multi-source verification before committing
skill_candidates.evidence: Discussion of Logan's Port required confirmation of multiple employers (Tyson Chicken, healthcare providers) rather than single dominant employer; Justin emphasized 'your biggest kicker' is understanding job security for tertiary markets

skill_candidates.change: Require utility bills and actual operating expense documentation, walking away from deals where sellers refuse to provide verification
skill_candidates.evidence: Josh's case study: seller wouldn't provide water bills, gave made-up T12 numbers ($150 vs. actual ~$700/month), prompting Josh to exit; Justin validated: 'common 101' that utilities must be verified and 'this affects his price'

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - Live Multifamily Deal Analysis_09-16-25 | Justin Brennan
